title: Kubernetes vs. OpenShift: Choosing DevOps and CI/CD Workflows
summary: IBM Technology compares Kubernetes and OpenShift as container platforms for DevOps and CI/CD workflows. The video discusses how platform choices affect deployment pipelines, operations, and delivery speed.
writeup: This short explainer contrasts Kubernetes and OpenShift by positioning Kubernetes as the container scheduling and networking engine and OpenShift (OCP) as a full platform layer on top of it, covering CI/CD pipelines, operators, and pre-built configurations. The core argument is that raw Kubernetes forces a team to assemble its own platform from separate tools, while OpenShift bundles that work into a single, integrated flow. The talk walks through the stack from bare metal or cloud up through the OS, Kubernetes, and OCP, then traces both the developer path and the operations path through a running cluster.
key_points: Running Kubernetes alone means building an entire platform yourself: CI/CD pipelines, security controls, and admission tooling must each be sourced and wired together separately, which OpenShift eliminates by providing them pre-integrated.
OpenShift supports every major substrate without reconfiguration: public cloud, private cloud, bare metal, and virtual machines all run the same OCP layer.

The developer flow in OpenShift is a single continuous chain: a code push triggers the built-in pipeline, the pipeline builds a container image, the image is pushed to a registry, and OpenShift deploys it through an image stream mechanism.

The operations side of OpenShift exposes a web console where the Ops team can monitor cluster resources, diagnose issues, and scale either by adding nodes (machines) or by increasing the pod count for a given application.
